seo.title: Vintage Turkish Chanakaleh Wool Rug | Noor Oriental Rugs
seo.description: Discover this rare, one-of-a-kind 2'10" x 4'1" hand-knotted vintage Turkish Chanakaleh wool rug, professionally restored and hand-washed with green shampoo.

description:
### Overview
This one-of-a-kind vintage Turkish Chanakaleh rug is an exquisite testament to traditional Anatolian hand-knotted craftsmanship. Woven entirely by hand less than 50 years ago, this piece showcases the distinctive geometric tribal design language native to the Chanakaleh region of Turkey.

### Design & Motifs
The rug's composition features a bold central mihrab-style architectural field in a rich, antique crimson red, populated by repeating diamond-shaped geometric medallions and stylized zoomorphic motifs outlined in deep navy blue. Intricate cream and soft golden ochre border bands enclose the central pattern, featuring clean zig-zag profiles and hand-woven geometric floral shapes that frame the entire field with striking symmetry.

### Materials & Construction
Constructed using a 100% natural wool warp and weft foundation topped with a low-profile, dense wool pile, this vintage area rug offers exceptional durability and an authentic, rustic underfoot texture. The pigments are colorfast, showcasing beautiful organic dye variations that have softened gracefully over time, giving the rug a distinguished patina and character.

### Restoration & Condition
To ensure the highest standards of hygiene and structural integrity, this piece has been professionally hand-washed with organic green shampoo and meticulously restored, preserving its fringes and side bindings for decades of continued enjoyment.

### Ideal Placement
With its compact dimensions of 2'10" × 4'1", this versatile accent rug is perfectly suited to bring warmth, history, and character to sophisticated interior spaces. It makes a statement foundation piece in a private library, executive home office, entry foyer, reading room, study, or bedside landing.

### Why This Rug is Special
As an authentic piece of Turkish weaving heritage, this one-of-a-kind rug effortlessly bridges the gap between historical lineage and modern luxury home aesthetics. Every knot tells a story of the artisan's touch, making it a unique collector's piece for those who appreciate the enduring beauty of traditional handmade wool rugs.
story:
### The Chanakaleh Weaving Tradition

Hailing from the northwestern region of Turkey near the historic Dardanelles, this piece embodies the rich heritage of the Chanakaleh weaving tradition. For generations, local artisans have hand-woven these unique Anatolian rugs, transforming raw wool into stories told in knots. A traditional Chanakaleh rug is highly sought after by collectors for its thick, double-knotted structure and distinct geometric tribal motifs that represent northwestern Anatolia. Unlike the floral patterns of urban workshops, Chanakaleh rugs express the authentic, rustic charm of rural village life.

### Design, Symbolism & Materials

The composition of this hand knotted Turkish rug centers on a bold mihrab-style layout, traditionally symbolizing a sacred arch or gateway. Inside this field, geometric diamond medallions and stylized zoomorphic motifs are woven in deep indigo blue, cream, and soft golden ochre. The construction uses 100% natural wool for both the warp foundation and the weft pile, providing exceptional insulation, sound dampening, and resilience. As a handmade wool rug, the natural lanolin in the wool keeps the fibers soft while offering natural stain resistance, ensuring the rug ages beautifully.

### Restoration & Heritage Preservation

Every vintage Turkish rug represents a unique chapter of cultural history. Because they are hand-knotted rather than machine-made, no two pieces are ever identical. To preserve this Oriental rug for future generations, the piece has been professionally hand-washed with organic shampoo and meticulously restored. This delicate restoration process reinforces the edges and secures the fringes without compromising the integrity of the original weave. This careful preservation ensures that the rug remains not just a home accessory, but a durable work of art ready to withstand decades of continued use.

seo.title: Antique Turkmen Tribal Gul Rug, 3' x 4'7" | Noor Oriental Rugs
seo.description: Hand-knotted antique Turkmen rug with geometric guls in red, indigo, and ivory wool. 3' x 4'7", one-of-a-kind. View details at Noor Oriental Rugs.

description:
### Overview
This antique Turkmen tribal rug is a compact, richly patterned example of the gul medallion tradition long associated with the nomadic weaving cultures of Central Asia. Measuring approximately 3 feet by 4 feet 7 inches, the rug is hand-knotted in wool and presents rows of octagonal gul medallions across a deep madder-red field, framed by narrow geometric guard borders and a band of stylized botehs along one end. Catalogued in our inventory as Rug No. 11117, it is offered as an antique piece.

### Design & Motifs
The field is organized in the classic Turkmen manner: columns of stacked octagonal guls, each quartered internally and outlined in indigo, set against a warm brick-red ground. This repeating medallion, known as the gul (from the Persian word for "flower" or "rose"), functioned historically as a kind of woven signature — different Turkmen tribal groups, among them the Tekke, Yomut, Ersari, and Salor, each favored their own gul geometry. The specific tribal attribution of this piece has not been confirmed (see Missing Information). A narrow band of stylized botehs runs along one end, while the guard borders carry a repeating rosette-and-diamond lattice, both common framing devices in Turkmen weaving.

### Materials & Construction
The rug is hand-knotted, a construction method clearly visible in close-up photography of the reverse side, where individual knot rows are legible through the foundation. The pile appears to be hand-spun wool, consistent with traditional Turkmen practice, though the specific fiber content of the warp and weft has not been independently verified for this catalog entry. Historically, Turkmen weavers worked largely from local materials — wool from their own flocks and dyes drawn from madder root, indigo, and other plants — and this rug's saturated, slightly variegated palette is consistent with that tradition, though whether its dyes are natural or synthetic has not been confirmed.

### Condition & Restoration
The rug presents in good, presentable condition consistent with its age. Photography of the reverse shows a canvas reinforcement strip along one edge, suggesting some prior conservation work, though formal restoration records are not on file for this piece. The fringe is intact and the corners lie flat. A complete condition report, including any professional washing or restoration history, is listed as outstanding in the Missing Information section.

### Ideal Placement
At roughly 3' x 4'7", this rug suits smaller footprints — an entryway, a reading nook, beside a bed, or layered atop a larger neutral rug in a living room. Its saturated red tone and dense geometric pattern make it a natural accent in spaces with simpler, more neutral surrounding decor, where the gul pattern can read clearly rather than compete with other busy elements.

### Why This Rug Is Special
Small-format antique Turkmen rugs like this one are increasingly scarce, and each carries the visual signature of a specific weaving lineage in its gul design. Beyond its decorative appeal, this piece is a handmade record of a textile tradition recognized by UNESCO as an element of intangible cultural heritage — one where pattern, color, and technique were passed down through generations rather than mass-produced. For collectors and design-conscious buyers alike, it offers both a functional accent rug and a tangible piece of Central Asian weaving history.
story:
### The Turkmen Weaving Tradition

Turkmen rugs originate from the nomadic and semi-nomadic Turkmen tribal confederations of Central Asia, a region spanning present-day Turkmenistan and extending into parts of Afghanistan, Uzbekistan, and northern Iran. For centuries, weaving was a central domestic craft among Turkmen women, who produced not only large-format rugs but also tent furnishings and trappings — door hangings, storage bags, tent bands — all made on portable looms suited to a mobile way of life.

### Design, Symbolism & Materials

The most recognizable feature of Turkmen weaving is the gul, a repeating medallion whose name derives from the Persian word for "flower" or "rose," even though the motif itself is almost always geometric and octagonal rather than floral. Historically, each major Turkmen tribal grouping — among them the Tekke, Yomut, Salor, Ersari, and Saryk — developed its own distinctive gul geometry, to the point that specialists can often identify which tribal group produced a given rug by examining the proportions and internal structure of its medallions. In this sense, the gul functioned as a woven tribal signature embedded into everyday textiles.

### Color in traditional Turkmen weaving

Color in traditional Turkmen weaving came from locally available materials: wool sheared from the weavers' own flocks, and dyes derived from plants such as madder root (for the deep reds that dominate most Turkmen rugs) and indigo (for blues), supplemented with other natural, and in later periods synthetic, dyestuffs. The resulting palette — saturated reds grounding cooler blues and warm ivory highlights — remains one of the most identifiable characteristics of the tradition.

### Trade History & The Bukhara Label

Much of what the West came to call "Bokhara" rugs are, in fact, Turkmen weavings that passed through the historic Silk Road trading city of Bukhara (in present-day Uzbekistan) on their way to international markets, rather than rugs woven in Bukhara itself. This trade history explains the persistence of "Bokhara" as a market label for Turkmen-style rugs even today.

### Cultural Heritage & UNESCO

In 2019, traditional Turkmen carpet weaving was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity — formal recognition of a craft practice passed down through generations for over a thousand years. Each piece, including smaller-format rugs like this one, represents an individual weaver's execution of an inherited design vocabulary, making every antique Turkmen rug both a functional textile and a document of a specific place, tribal lineage, and moment within a living weaving tradition.

seo.title: Yağcıbedir Turkish Rug, 3'4" x 3'5" | Noor Oriental Rugs
seo.description: Hand-knotted Yağcıbedir Turkish rug with a geometric medallion and bird motifs in red, ivory, and navy wool. 3'4" x 3'5", one-of-a-kind. View details at Noor Oriental Rugs.

description:
### Overview
This rug is a hand-knotted example of the Yağcıbedir weaving tradition of Western Anatolia, Turkey. Measuring 3'4" x 3'5" — a nearly square format historically associated with dowry pieces — it presents a stepped octagonal medallion in madder red and indigo on an ivory ground, populated with stylized birds and paired ram-horn (koçboynuzu) motifs, and framed by concentric geometric guard borders. Catalogued in our inventory as Rug No. 303, its intake tag lists the design as "GEO" (geometric) and the quality grade as "Super."

### Design & Motifs
The field is centered on a large stepped medallion outlined in a red-and-gold zigzag band, containing a navy ground scattered with diamond lattice, small paired birds, and hooked ram-horn forms rendered in red, brown, and ivory. The corners of the field carry secondary geometric devices in dark brown and cream, echoing motifs used elsewhere in Western Anatolian weaving. The outer borders combine a wide madder-red band of stepped diamonds with narrower guard stripes in cream, gold, and navy — a restrained, high-contrast palette characteristic of Yağcıbedir work.

### Materials & Construction
The rug is hand-knotted in wool, with individual knots and the reverse foundation clearly visible in the supplied macro and reverse-side photography. Traditional Yağcıbedir weaving is known for a notably high knot density and wool that is hand-spun and naturally dyed by the weavers themselves; whether this specific piece follows natural or synthetic dyeing has not been confirmed. The fiber content of the warp and weft has likewise not been independently verified for this catalog entry.

### Condition & Restoration
The rug presents in good condition in the supplied photography, with an intact fringe and no visible major damage, staining, or repair. No formal condition report, cleaning history, or restoration record was included with the intake materials, so this should be confirmed before publishing a specific condition claim.

### Ideal Placement
At roughly 3'4" x 3'5", this nearly square rug suits an entryway, in front of a fireplace, beside a bed, or as an accent within a larger seating arrangement. Its high-contrast red, ivory, and navy palette and dense geometric medallion make it a strong focal point in simpler, more neutral surroundings.

### Why This Rug Is Special
Yağcıbedir rugs are a distinct, named weaving tradition tied to a specific cluster of villages in the Sındırgı district of Balıkesir province — not a generic label, but a recognizable regional style prized for its disciplined red-white-navy color scheme and fine, dense knotting. Originally woven as dowry pieces, each carries a domestic, personal history within the weaving culture it comes from. A well-preserved, nearly square example like this one offers both a strong graphic presence and a tangible connection to a specific, documented Anatolian weaving village tradition.
story:
### The Yağcıbedir Weaving Tradition
Yağcıbedir is not the name of a single town but a label for a distinct type of carpet woven by Yörük (nomadic Turkish) weavers in and around Sındırgı, in the Balıkesir province of Western Anatolia. The tradition is associated with a specific cluster of villages — among them Esmedere, Çakıllı, Karakaya, Alacamescit, and Eğridere — where weaving was passed down through generations as a domestic, village-based craft rather than a large commercial workshop industry.

Historically, Yağcıbedir rugs were woven primarily as dowry pieces: textiles a young woman would weave and bring into her marriage, intended for use within the home rather than for immediate sale. This domestic origin shaped the format of many surviving pieces, which are often small to medium in size — including the nearly square, compact proportions seen in this rug — rather than room-sized carpets woven for a trade market.

The most distinctive feature of Yağcıbedir weaving is its restrained, high-contrast color palette: madder red, undyed ivory or cream wool, and deep indigo-navy, occasionally set off with small amounts of brown or gold. Traditionally, weavers spun their own wool and dyed it using natural, plant-based dyes, a practice tied to the same local, self-sufficient production methods used throughout rural Anatolian weaving. Designs are geometric rather than curvilinear, built from stepped medallions, hooked border devices, and stylized figures — including birds and the paired "ram's horn" (koçboynuzu) motif seen in this rug — that recur throughout Turkish tribal and village weaving as symbols of fertility, protection, and continuity, though their precise original meanings vary by region and are not uniformly documented.

Yağcıbedir carpets are also noted among specialists for their unusually high knot density relative to other village weavings of the region, a technical hallmark that produces the crisp, precise geometry visible in well-preserved examples. As with many named regional weaving traditions, the label "Yağcıbedir" is sometimes applied loosely in the trade to rugs woven in a similar style outside the original village cluster; the specific workshop or village origin of this particular rug has not been independently confirmed.

seo.title: Royal Bokhara Rug, 4'2" x 5'11" | Noor Oriental Rugs
seo.description: Hand-knotted Royal Bokhara rug with a classic windowpane medallion design in rose-pink and charcoal wool. 4'2" x 5'11". View details at Noor Oriental Rugs.

description:
### Overview
This rug is woven in the Royal Bokhara pattern, a well-known design within the broader Bokhara (Turkmen-derived gul) family of rugs. It presents three columns of elongated octagon medallions, each quartered by intersecting lines, repeated in even rows across a soft rose-pink field and framed by a wide, densely patterned charcoal-and-black border with multiple guard stripes. At 4'2" x 5'11", it is a mid-size room accent. No inventory tag with a rug number, price, or quality grade was included with the supplied materials for this piece.

### Design & Motifs
The field carries the design that gives the "Royal" Bokhara its name: rows of elongated octagons, each divided into quarters by crossing lines, alternating with small cross-shaped diamond devices between medallions. This differs from the "Princess" Bokhara, a smaller prayer-rug format in the same design family. The wide border combines several guard stripes of repeating rosettes and diamond lattice with a bold outer band of stepped diagonal motifs, all rendered in dark charcoal, black, and muted rose against the field's warm pink-rose ground.

### Materials & Construction
The rug is hand-knotted, with the pile and knot structure visible in the close-up photography supplied. Rugs of this pattern are most commonly woven with a wool pile on a cotton foundation, a combination widely used in Pakistani production of the Bokhara design since the mid-20th century; the specific fiber content, foundation material, and country of origin for this individual piece have not been confirmed.

### Condition & Restoration
The rug presents as clean and even in the supplied photography, with no visible damage, staining, or obvious repair. No formal condition report, cleaning history, or restoration record was included with the intake materials.

### Ideal Placement
At 4'2" x 5'11", this rug suits a small living room seating area, a den, a bedroom, or a hallway. Its warm rose-pink ground and dark, dense border read as a classic, versatile neutral in traditional and transitional interiors alike.

### Why This Rug Is Special
The Royal Bokhara windowpane pattern is one of the most recognizable design families in the oriental rug trade, tracing its motif lineage to Turkmen gul medallions and popularized through 20th-century Pakistani weaving production. A well-executed example like this one offers the crisp, orderly geometry and restrained palette that has made this pattern a long-standing favorite for traditional interiors.
story:
### The Bokhara Weaving Tradition
"Bokhara" (also spelled Bukhara) takes its name from the historic Silk Road city of Bukhara, in present-day Uzbekistan — a major trading center through which Central Asian carpets passed on their way to international markets. In the rug trade, however, "Bokhara" refers less to rugs actually woven in that city and more to a design tradition: repeating gul medallions originally associated with Turkmen tribal weaving, most recognizably the Tekke tribe's octagonal gul.

The specific "Royal Bokhara" pattern seen in this rug — rows of elongated octagons, each quartered by crossing lines — is a named design within this family, distinguished in the trade from the "Princess Bokhara," a smaller prayer-rug format woven with a similar medallion vocabulary.

Much of the Bokhara-pattern production familiar in the West today was, and continues to be, woven not in Central Asia but in Pakistan. Following the partition of British India in 1947, Muslim weavers who relocated to Pakistan worked alongside Turkmen weaving communities, and the Pakistani government established training programs and organized workshops to build a carpet-weaving export industry. Because of its comparatively straightforward, repeating structure, the Bokhara gul design was among the first patterns used to train weavers in these programs, and it went on to become one of the most widely produced and recognized rug patterns associated with Pakistani weaving — commonly woven with a wool pile, sometimes blended with New Zealand wool, on a cotton foundation.

As a result, a rug in the Royal Bokhara pattern may originate from a range of production contexts, and the specific weaving origin of this individual piece has not been confirmed.

description:
### Overview
This rug is woven in the Kazak style associated with the Caucasus region. It presents two columns of large octagonal medallions, each centered on a stepped diamond of red, navy, ivory, and brown, arranged across a warm brown field and separated by small cruciform devices. A wide border of eight-pointed stars and diagonal diamond bands frames the field. Measuring 4'2" x 6'1", it is catalogued in our inventory as Rug No. 697.

### Design & Motifs
The field is built from bold, large-scale octagonal medallions, each containing a stepped diamond "gul" rendered in concentric bands of red, navy, and ivory around a checkerboard-patterned core. Small cross- and diamond-shaped devices fill the space between medallions. This kind of large, high-contrast geometric medallion is characteristic of Kazak-group weaving from the Caucasus, where bold scale and saturated color are prioritized over fine detail. The border combines a band of eight-pointed stars with a narrower diagonal "reciprocal" stripe, both common framing devices in Caucasian rugs.

### Materials & Construction
The rug is hand-knotted, with individual knots and pile structure visible in the supplied macro photography. Kazak-group rugs are traditionally woven with hand-spun wool pile on a wool foundation, using a coarser, lower knot count than city workshop rugs, which contributes to their bold, graphic look and durable hand. The pile, warp, and weft of this individual piece are 100% lamb wool.

### Condition & Restoration
The rug presents as clean and even in the supplied photography, with intact fringe and no visible damage or repair. It has been professionally washed with green organic shampoo and restored, and is in good condition.

### Ideal Placement
At 4'2" x 6'1", this rug suits a living room seating area, a dining room, or a den. Its bold, high-contrast medallions and warm brown ground make it a strong focal point in rooms with simpler, more neutral furnishings.

### Why This Rug Is Special
Kazak rugs are among the most recognizable and sought-after tribal weavings from the Caucasus, prized for their bold scale, saturated palette, and confident geometry. A well-preserved example like this one offers a graphic, room-defining presence along with a tie to one of the best-known regional weaving traditions in the oriental rug trade.
story:
### The Kazak Weaving Tradition
"Kazak" refers to a broad group of rugs woven in the southwestern Caucasus, a mountainous region spanning parts of present-day Armenia, Georgia, and Azerbaijan, and takes its name from the town of Kazakh (Qazax) in modern Azerbaijan, a historic weaving and trading center in the 19th and early 20th centuries. Caucasian weaving in general — of which Kazak is one of the best-known regional styles — developed among village and tribal communities working on relatively simple looms, producing rugs for domestic use, local trade, and eventually for export through regional market towns.

### Design, Scale & Motifs
Kazak rugs are distinguished by their bold scale: large geometric medallions, hooked diamonds, and cross motifs rendered in a small number of saturated colors, with a comparatively coarse, low knot count that favors strong graphic impact over fine detail. Many of the recurring devices found in Kazak and other Caucasian weaving — stars, wheel-like medallions, hooked "ram's horn" motifs, and running-water border patterns — are drawn from a shared symbolic vocabulary found across the region's textiles and metalwork, though the precise original meaning of any single motif is not always documented and can vary by community.

The Caucasus has historically sat at a crossroads of Persian, Turkish, and Russian cultural influence, and its rug weaving absorbed elements from each while retaining a distinct regional character built on geometric abstraction rather than the curvilinear, floral vocabulary of Persian court weaving. Within the broader Kazak group, specialists further distinguish named sub-styles by district and medallion type (for example, the Karachopf Kazak, known for its star-like central medallion) — a specific sub-attribution for this rug has not been confirmed.
